query: A gauge has a historically stable measurement spread from years of calibration. After a repair, 64 readings average slightly above the target. The engineer asks whether the process center has moved upward, not whether the spread changed.

The process variation is treated as known from a long control history. A new random sample reports only n and xbar, and the task is to give a symmetric two-sided uncertainty statement for the true mean.
Only twelve observations are available, the population spread is not known, and a normal model is explicitly assumed. The question asks whether the mean exceeds the contractual value.
A laboratory reports xbar and s from 18 approximately normal measurements. The requested confidence statement is for the underlying center, not for the variability.

A manufacturer claims that the instrument variability is below a tolerance. An analyst repeats the same measurement several times and obtains only a sample standard deviation. The mean reading is irrelevant to the claim.
For a normal population, the report gives n and s squared and asks for a confidence interval for the true standard deviation. No comparison with another sample is involved.

A right-tailed test of a claimed maximum standard deviation is fixed at alpha before sampling. The problem then asks for the probability of rejection when the true standard deviation is twice the claimed value.
The lab wants a test of a maximum allowed variance to detect a larger true variance with probability at least 0.95. The significance level is fixed before any readings are taken.

Two suppliers have the same nominal mean concentration, but the question is whether their standard deviations differ by a practically meaningful factor. Independent normal samples from both suppliers are summarized by s1 and s2.

Before comparing two process averages, the analyst is asked to check whether the two normal populations can plausibly be treated as having the same variance. Two independent sample variances are given.
Two independent formulations are tested, and long experience says both have the same known standard deviation. The new formulation is supposed to reduce the mean response time.
For two independent groups with common known standard deviation, the experiment is planned so that a specified difference in means will be detected with high probability at a fixed alpha level.
Two independent normal samples are collected from processes whose variability is considered stable and common. The practical question is whether replacing the old catalyst changes the mean response.
Two independent small normal samples have no known population standard deviation, but engineering experience supports a common variance. The requested output is an interval for mu1 minus mu2.
Two independent samples come from different machines with visibly different sample spreads. No equality of variances may be assumed, but the question is still about a difference in means.
The data consist of two unrelated groups of unequal size. Both sample standard deviations are reported and differ substantially, while the problem asks whether the population means differ.
Each participant is measured once under a baseline condition and once under a treatment condition. The question is whether the treatment increases the average paired response.
Ten specimens are each measured with both instruments. The requested confidence interval concerns the average difference between instruments, not two unrelated population means.
A before-after study produces paired differences with several outliers. The claim is about whether the typical post-treatment value is larger, and a distribution-free signed procedure is requested.
For paired measurements, the differences appear roughly symmetric but the sample size is too small to rely on normality. The hypothesis concerns a nonzero median difference.
Only the number of observations above and below the claimed median is retained; the magnitudes of the deviations are discarded. The task is to test whether the center is larger than claimed.

A batch is acceptable only if the long-run defective fraction is not above a specified value. A large sample gives the number of defectives, and the question asks whether the supplier's claim should be rejected.
A survey reports x successes out of n trials and asks for a two-sided uncertainty interval for the underlying success probability. The sample is large enough for the normal approximation.
Two independent treatment groups report the number of successes and failures. The question is whether the success probability is higher under the new treatment.
A company compares customer complaint rates before and after a policy change using two independent samples. It asks for an interval for the change in proportions rather than a single p-value.
Before inspecting a shipment, buyer and supplier agree on a rejection number for defectives so that good batches are rarely rejected and bad batches are accepted with controlled probability.

A cutoff for the sample mean is chosen before data arrive. The problem asks for both the probability of rejecting under the null boundary and the probability of rejecting under a specified alternative.
A test produces p = 0.003 after observing a sample mean beyond the null boundary. The question asks which verbal interpretation of this number is correct.
The task asks only how surprising the observed statistic is under the null model and explicitly avoids fixing a loss function or an alternative value in advance.
The problem first asks for a p-value from the observed data, then separately asks for a pre-sampling rule with controlled type-I and type-II errors. Both interpretations must be distinguished.
Counts of events per unit are tabulated as frequencies for 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 or more events. The question asks whether a Poisson model is plausible after estimating its rate from the data.
A large sample of exam scores is grouped into bins. The mean and variance are estimated from the sample, and the task is to assess whether a normal distribution is an adequate model.
A table cross-classifies students by major and preferred study method. The question asks whether the two categorical variables are associated.
Observed frequencies are arranged by machine type and defect category, with row and column totals both random. The question is whether defect category depends on machine type.
A contingency table has several cells with very small expected counts. The problem asks whether the usual large-sample chi-square approximation is reliable.
For paired quantitative measurements assumed to be sampled from a bivariate normal population, the sample correlation is reported and the task is to test whether the population correlation is zero.
A study reports r from n paired observations and asks for a confidence interval for the underlying Pearson correlation, not for a regression slope.

Two unrelated samples give one measurement each, but the question is about association between two numerical variables. The setup lacks paired observations, so a correlation analysis is not justified.

A manufacturer's claim is written as 'not more than' a threshold, while the analyst wants evidence that the claim fails. The correct tail is determined by the alternative, not by the wording of the claim alone.

Instead of reporting a p-value, the solution uses a confidence interval and checks whether the null boundary lies outside it. The problem asks whether this is equivalent to the corresponding two-sided test.
Two independent samples are small, strongly skewed, and the question concerns whether one population tends to produce larger values than the other. Pairing is not present.
Measurements come from two different groups of subjects rather than before-after differences on the same subject. A rank-based alternative to the two-sample t procedure is requested.
For matched pairs, both the signs and the ranks of absolute differences are used. Zero differences are discarded before ranking.
The only safe assumption is continuity around the hypothesized median; the distribution may be highly skewed. The test should use only whether observations fall above or below the proposed median.
The same paired dataset is analyzed by a paired mean procedure, a signed-rank procedure, and a sign-only procedure. The question asks which assumptions justify each method.
The table lists Ball X and Ball Y measurements for each specimen. Treating the two columns as unrelated samples would throw away the blocking information.
The problem asks to compare the conclusions from an unequal-variance mean test and a rank-based two-sample test, including what population features each method targets.
Two small independent samples are plausibly normal and have comparable spreads. The course solution pools the variance before testing the difference in means.
The prompt says the two population variances should not be assumed equal. The statistic still compares two independent sample means and uses an approximate degree of freedom.
A preliminary question asks whether two independent normal populations have equal variance, because the answer affects whether a pooled comparison of means is defensible.
A sample of manufactured items yields a count of failures. The conclusion concerns whether the population failure fraction exceeds the contractual limit.
Two factories each inspect a large number of units and record defectives. Management asks whether the defect rates differ after the process change.
A die-like device produces counts in six categories. The null model specifies the full vector of category probabilities, and the task is to compare observed and expected frequencies.
Patients are classified by treatment group and outcome category. The question is whether outcome distribution is independent of treatment group.
A theoretical distribution is fitted to grouped data by estimating one parameter from the same sample before computing expected counts. The degrees of freedom must be reduced accordingly.
A rectangular contingency table has r rows and c columns. The problem asks for the degrees of freedom used in the association test.
Under the null median, positive and negative deviations should occur with equal probability. The resulting count statistic is compared to a binomial distribution.
The wording is that one distribution is shifted to the right of the other, not that the sample averages differ. Independent observations from both groups are ranked together.
After computing paired differences, nearly all nonzero differences are positive and their absolute magnitudes are ranked. The alternative is that the second condition tends to be larger.
The prompt gives before and after values but the rows have been shuffled so individual matching is lost. A paired signed-rank calculation would no longer be valid.
A simple linear model is fitted to response versus one quantitative predictor. The question asks whether the predictor has a nonzero linear effect after estimating the slope and residual variance.
For a simple linear regression, the fitted slope and its standard error are reported. The requested result is a confidence interval for the expected change in response per unit increase in x.
With exactly one predictor, the ANOVA table is used to test whether the regression explains a significant amount of variation. This is equivalent to testing the single slope.
A multiple-choice item asks what a large value of R squared actually means. The correct statement is about explained observed variation, not proof of the true model or guaranteed prediction accuracy.
After fitting a line, the problem asks for an interval for a single future measurement at x0. The random future error must be included.
A fitted simple regression model is used to estimate the average response at a specified x0. The interval should not include the extra noise of one future observation.
The residuals versus fitted values form a funnel shape. The question asks whether the constant variance assumption for the regression errors is credible.
Several observations are taken at the same x-values, allowing pure error to be separated from model error. The question asks whether a straight-line mean function is adequate.
A regression dataset has no repeated predictor settings. The problem asks why the usual pure-error lack-of-fit decomposition cannot be performed.
A model with several predictors is fitted, and the question asks whether at least one non-intercept coefficient is nonzero. The output includes SSR, SSE, and degrees of freedom.

A full regression model includes four predictors, while a reduced model removes two of them. The task is to test whether those two deleted predictors jointly contribute after the others remain in the model.
The fitted model contains x and x squared. A multiple-choice question asks what can be concluded from a significant overall regression test when R squared is close to one.
The problem describes a regression with an intercept, one numerical predictor, and a two-level group indicator. It asks for the design matrix used in least squares.
Using the model specification matrix X, the fitted vector is written as HY with H = X(X'X)^(-1)X'. The question asks for the geometric meaning of this operation.
The proof asks why SSR and SSE are independent under the normal linear model by decomposing the response into orthogonal projection components.
Several candidate regression models have similar R squared values. The criterion requested is based on leave-one-out prediction errors rather than in-sample fit.
A model with more predictors has lower SSE but worse leave-one-out predictive performance. The question asks which indicator would reveal this overfitting.
Adding a useless predictor cannot decrease ordinary R squared, so the question asks for a model comparison quantity that accounts for the number of predictors.
A fitted relationship between density and strength is statistically significant. The question asks what can and cannot be concluded about causation and prediction from the regression output.
After fitting a linear regression with p predictors, the problem asks for the estimator of the error variance using SSE and the appropriate residual degrees of freedom.
A straight-line model is compared to a model that also includes a squared predictor. The task is to test whether the curvature term significantly improves the fit.
The requested prediction is made far outside the observed range of x-values. The calculation may be formal, but the interpretation should warn about extrapolation.
Repeated observations at each mass value allow comparing deviations from fitted line to deviations from within-setting means. The question asks whether the linear calibration relation is appropriate.

The data consist of paired quantitative observations and the question asks for a test of linear association; the same evidence could be expressed through correlation or the slope in simple regression.
Candidate models differ by interaction and polynomial terms. The task is to choose a parsimonious model using predictive indicators rather than simply maximizing in-sample fit.
Two independent normal samples are given. The first subquestion asks whether the spreads differ; the second asks how that result affects the subsequent comparison of means.

A manufacturer claims a mean exceeds a threshold, but the formal null is written as the complement of that claim. The observed sample average is only useful after translating the claim into a tail direction.
The same normal sample is used first to build a confidence interval for the mean and then to decide whether a two-sided null value is plausible at the matching significance level.
A quality-control problem asks for a rejection rule against excessive variability, the power at a larger true spread, and the minimum n needed to achieve a target power.
Each unit is tested under two conditions, and the exam asks for a paired mean test, a signed-rank test, and a comparison of their assumptions and conclusions.
Two unrelated groups are compared. The problem offers a pooled mean test, an unequal-variance mean test, and a rank-sum alternative, then asks which assumptions make each acceptable.
A simple regression problem asks for the fitted line, an estimate of error variance, a significance test for regression, R squared interpretation, and a prediction interval at a specified x value.
The same predictor levels are observed by two groups, enabling a regression fit and then a lack-of-fit test based on pure error. The conclusion concerns appropriateness of the linear model.
The problem gives frequency counts and asks first whether they fit a specified distribution, then in a separate table asks whether two categorical classifications are independent.

A shipment is accepted unless the number of defectives in a sample reaches a preassigned cutoff. The exam asks to choose the cutoff from a producer-risk constraint and then compute consumer risk at a worse defect rate.
A paired numerical dataset is summarized by r, a fitted simple regression line, and an ANOVA table. The questions ask for association, slope significance, and interpretation of explained variation.
For the same fitted regression at x0, the exam asks for both an interval for the mean response and an interval for one future observation, then asks why one is wider.
A multiple regression task compares nested models by an F statistic and then asks which model would be preferred using a prediction-oriented indicator such as PRESS.
The problem contrasts reporting a small p-value after seeing the data with designing a rule that controls alpha and beta before seeing the data. The distinction itself is being tested.

A final-review item lists several short scenarios: known spread mean claim, unknown spread mean claim, variance claim, paired design, and contingency table. The task is to map each scenario to the correct procedure without computing final numerical answers.

A password is made by selecting several different symbols from a larger set, and the order in which the symbols are written matters. The question asks for the number of possible passwords, not a probability model yet.
A committee is selected from a class, and only the membership matters. No role such as chair or secretary is assigned.
A sequence of trials is summarized only by how many times each of several labels occurred. The labels within each group are indistinguishable, but the positions in the sequence matter.

Two quality alarms can both be triggered by the same item. The problem gives the separate alarm probabilities and the probability that both alarms trigger, then asks for the probability that at least one alarm triggers.
Two events cannot occur together, but each has positive probability. The question asks whether one can still call them independent.
After event A is reported to have occurred, the stated probability of event B is unchanged from its original probability. The task is to express this as a mathematical condition.
A screening test has high sensitivity and specificity, but the disease is rare in the population. The task asks for the chance that a positive patient is actually diseased.
Several factories contribute different proportions of production, and defective items are disproportionately traced back to one factory. The question asks for the defect rate within each factory.
A population is split into exhaustive non-overlapping groups. For each group the probability of a failure is known, and the question asks for the overall failure probability.
The direct event is that at least two people share a birthday. The efficient route is to first count the probability that all birthdays are distinct.
A host deliberately reveals a losing option after the contestant has made an initial choice. The question asks whether switching changes the winning probability.

A family has two children and one child is known to have a specified property that occurs with probability p. The task asks how that extra description changes the chance that both children share a sex.
A fair coin is tossed until the first head appears. The sample space includes the infinite all-tails sequence, and the question asks how to treat its probability.
Candidates are observed sequentially and rejected choices cannot be recalled. The proposed strategy is to skip an initial fraction and then accept the first record afterward.
A two-way table gives counts by exposure status and disease outcome. The question asks for the probability of exposure among diseased individuals, not disease among exposed individuals.
A test is described as 99 percent accurate, yet the condition is extremely uncommon. The interpretation question asks why most positive results may still be false positives.
Two Bernoulli-style trials do not influence each other, but their success probabilities are different. The task asks which binomial assumption fails.
Balls are drawn from a finite box without replacement. The second draw's success probability changes after seeing the first draw.
A fixed number of identical independent inspections is performed, and the response variable is the number of defective items found.
Independent trials continue until the first successful transmission. The response variable is the number of trials required.
A software interface reports the number of failed login attempts before the first successful login, rather than the total number of attempts.
A repeated experiment continues until the fifth success occurs, and the random variable is the total number of trials used.
A process is observed until r accepted items are produced; the random variable counts rejected items before that stopping time.
Calls arrive randomly at a constant average rate, and the question asks for the number of calls in the next fixed time interval.
A sample is drawn from a finite shipment containing a known number of defective units, without replacing inspected units.
A random digit generator is assumed to produce each integer in a finite list with the same probability.
A measurement error is equally likely anywhere between two endpoints and has density zero outside that interval.
A component has a constant failure rate and the variable of interest is the waiting time until failure.
A device has already survived ten hours, and the question asks for the distribution of the additional waiting time under a constant hazard model.
Several independent exponential waiting times with the same rate are added to model the time until the kth arrival.
Repeated physical measurements fluctuate symmetrically around a target with a known standard deviation, and the question asks for a tail probability.
The problem asks for a cutoff exceeded by only a specified upper-tail probability under a normal model.
The statistic is formed by summing squared standardized normal observations and the task asks for its reference distribution.
A standardized sample mean uses the sample standard deviation from a normal population instead of the population standard deviation.
Two independent sample variances from normal populations are compared by taking their ratio after scaling by the hypothesized variances.
A count model is suggested, and the diagnostic hint is that the theoretical mean and variance should be equal.
The number of successes in n independent identical trials is modeled, and the question asks for the mean and variance of that count.
The waiting time to first success is used, and the problem asks for its expectation through either a series or moment-generating function.
The waiting time for r successes is decomposed as a sum of r independent waiting times for one success each.
A heavy-tailed density is symmetric around a center, but the integral defining the expectation fails to converge absolutely.
A payoff doubles each time the first success is delayed, and each possible delay contributes the same positive amount to the expected value.
The key clue is the support: a density proportional to x on the positive half-line with an exponential term in x squared.
The wording alternates between number of arrivals during a fixed interval and time until the next arrival, so the selected distribution changes.
A coin is tossed thousands of times and the question asks for the probability that the number of heads lies in a narrow interval around its mean.
A large binomial count is compared to an integer cutoff, and the approximation must shift the cutoff by one half before standardizing.
A large package contains many independent items, each with a very small defect probability. The task asks for the chance of at most a few defects.
The exact binomial probability of zero successes is awkward because n is large and p is tiny; the expected number of successes is moderate.
Many reservations each independently show up with the same probability, and the hotel asks for the chance that arrivals do not exceed capacity.
Individual observations are not known to be normal, but the sample size is large and the question concerns the distribution of the sample mean.
A sample proportion is compared with a hypothesized value using the approximate normal distribution of the count of successes.
Two independent groups report success counts large enough that both sample proportions can be compared by a normal approximation.
A Poisson count has a large expected value and the task asks for an interval probability where a normal approximation would be convenient.
The problem asks for the probability that the sum of many independent observations exceeds a threshold, not the average, but the same normal approximation idea applies.
A proposed normal approximation to a binomial model is questionable because one of np and n(1-p) is small.
The sample size is large, but the event probability is so small that the expected count remains small; the problem asks which approximation is more natural.
A discrete random variable is transformed by a nonlinear function, and the expectation of the transformed variable is requested without first deriving the full transformed distribution.
A continuous random variable with known density is passed through a function g, and the problem asks for E[g(X)].
The second moment and mean are easy to compute, and the problem asks for the variance without expanding the squared deviation integral directly.
The expectation of a sum of random variables is needed, but no independence assumption is stated or required.
The variance of a sum is requested, and the wording explicitly states that the random variables are independent.
The variables are not independent, so the variance of their sum must include covariance terms.
A variable is centered by subtracting its mean and scaled by its standard deviation. The problem asks for the mean and variance after this operation.
A moment-generating function is provided explicitly, and the task asks for E[X] and Var[X].
The calculated moment-generating function matches the known form of a standard distribution, and the task asks for the distribution of the variable.
Two independent random variables are added and both moment-generating functions are known.
A proposed moment-generating function calculation ignores the interval of t values on which the expectation exists.
The loss function is absolute deviation rather than squared deviation, and the question asks which location parameter minimizes expected loss.
The loss is squared error around a chosen constant, and the task asks which constant minimizes expected loss.
Only the mean and variance are known, and the task asks for a guaranteed bound on the probability of being far from the mean.
The random variable is nonnegative and the problem asks for an upper bound on the probability that it exceeds a positive threshold.
Two variables have zero covariance, and the question asks whether that fact alone proves independence.
One variable is converted by adding a constant and multiplying by a positive or negative scalar, and the problem asks how correlation changes.
A nonnegative integer-valued random variable is easier to analyze by summing tail probabilities rather than by summing x times the mass function.
A continuous variable is transformed by a one-to-one increasing function and the density of the transformed variable is requested.
A continuous variable is squared, so two preimages may contribute to the density of the transformed variable.
The transformation is trigonometric over an interval where the same output can arise from multiple input values.
A bivariate density is given on a triangular region and the task asks for the distribution of U=X/Y.
Two independent continuous variables are observed, and the response variable is their minimum.
Two independent continuous variables are observed, and the response variable is their maximum.
The density of a sum of two independent continuous variables is requested.
A joint density over a two-dimensional region is given, and the task asks for the density of one coordinate alone.
A joint density is compared with the product of the two marginal densities to decide whether two variables are independent.
The joint density of X and Y is known, and the problem asks for the density of Y after X is fixed at a value.
The joint density formula appears factorable, but the support is triangular rather than rectangular.
A joint continuous distribution is given and the question asks for covariance or correlation, requiring E[XY], E[X], and E[Y].
A two-dimensional normal density contains a cross term, and the task asks how the correlation coefficient appears in the density.
A vector of independent normal variables is multiplied by an invertible matrix, and the distribution of the transformed vector is requested.
A random sample is taken from a normal population, and the distribution of the sample mean is needed exactly, not approximately.
A random sample from a normal population is used to form (n-1)S^2/sigma^2.
The sample mean and sample variance are calculated from a normal sample, and the question asks about their independence.
The maximum of n independent uniform observations is used to estimate the endpoint of the distribution.
The requested transformed density has awkward inverse branches, so the safer method is to first compute P[g(X) <= z].
A pair of new variables is defined from X and Y by an invertible transformation, and the joint density must be adjusted by a determinant.
The problem frames centered random variables as vectors and asks for correlation as a normalized scalar product.
For a bivariate normal model specifically, zero correlation is used to conclude independence.
A system fails as soon as either one of two independent components fails. The component lifetimes are exponential.
A system continues working as long as at least one of two independent components is still alive.
The task asks for the probability that a component is still functioning after time t.
A reliability function is known and the problem asks for the density of the failure time.
The lifetime model has a failure rate that does not depend on age.
The system lifetime equals the minimum of independent component lifetimes.
The system lifetime equals the maximum of independent component lifetimes.
Two components are arranged in parallel and that block is then placed in series with a third component.
The formula multiplying survival probabilities is proposed, and the question asks what assumption makes it valid.
Observed exponential lifetimes are used to infer the rate parameter before reporting a reliability at a future time.
A histogram has a long right tail, and the question asks whether the mean should be expected to exceed the median.
A boxplot shows several points beyond the whiskers, and the task asks for the conventional rule used to flag them.
Two boxplots are placed side by side, and the question asks which group has greater variability and whether medians differ visually.
A data visualization task asks for a principled histogram bin width based on the sample size and interquartile range.
A sample variance is computed from data and the question asks why n-1 rather than n appears in the denominator.
One extreme observation is added to a dataset, and the question asks which summary, mean or median, changes more.
A diagnostic plot shows points bending away from a straight line in both tails, and the task asks whether normality is plausible.
A residual plot against fitted values shows a funnel shape rather than a constant vertical spread.
Measurements are collected over time and the plot shows long runs above and below the center.
A scatterplot shows an approximately straight increasing cloud with no strong curvature.
The distribution is highly skewed with extreme values, and the report asks for robust location and spread summaries.
Before running a formal two-sample procedure, the task asks for a plot that displays center, spread, skewness, and outliers by group.
A parameterized distribution has an expectation depending on theta, and the estimator is obtained by setting that expectation equal to the sample mean.
The first moment is inconvenient, so the estimator is obtained by matching E[X^2] to the sample second moment.
A likelihood is built from independent observations and the parameter estimate is found by maximizing its logarithm.
For observations from Uniform(0,theta), increasing theta lowers the density but theta must still be at least as large as the largest observation.
The density contains exp(-|x|/sigma), and the likelihood maximization reduces to the average absolute observation.
An estimator is judged by comparing its expectation to the true parameter value.
Two unbiased estimators are compared by how variable their sampling distributions are.
An estimator has nonzero bias, and the task asks for the criterion combining squared bias and variance.
The maximum-likelihood estimator is biased and a corrected multiple of it gives a smaller mean squared error.
The estimator may be biased at finite n, but the bias and variance vanish as the sample size grows.
The likelihood depends on the sample only through a small summary such as the sum or maximum of observations.
After estimating theta by maximum likelihood, the question asks for the MLE of a function g(theta).
The problem asks for two different estimators of the same parameter, one by matching moments and one by maximizing likelihood.
An estimator is known to systematically fall below the parameter by a multiplicative factor, and the task asks how to remove the bias.
Two method-of-moments estimators arise from matching two different moments, and the question asks whether they must be the same.
The support of the density changes with the parameter, so regular derivative-only likelihood arguments may miss the boundary maximum.
A statistic computed from repeated samples is itself random, and the task asks for its distribution under the assumed model.
The prompt asks for the best single parameter value suggested by the data, not for a reject-or-not decision.
A normal sample gives a sample variance, and the requested interval is for the population variance.
The question asks for a confidence interval for the population standard deviation rather than the variance itself.
A survey reports x successes out of n trials and asks for a two-sided uncertainty interval for the true success probability.
Two independent samples have known population standard deviations, and the task asks for an interval for mu1 minus mu2.
Two independent normal samples have unknown but assumed equal variances, and the task asks for a confidence interval for the difference in means.
Two independent small samples show noticeably different sample variances, and equal population variances are not assumed.
The same subjects are measured before and after treatment, and the requested interval is for the mean of the within-subject differences.
For a fitted simple linear regression, the task asks for uncertainty in the average response at a specified x value.
For a fitted regression line, the task asks for an interval for one future individual response at a specified x value.
A sample of exponential lifetimes is used to construct an interval for the rate parameter beta using a chi-square pivot.
Two confidence intervals use the same data but different confidence levels, and the task asks which one must be wider.
The desired margin of error is specified before sampling, and the task asks how large n must be for a mean interval.
The engineering requirement asks only for an upper bound on a failure probability or mean, not a two-sided interval.
A completed confidence interval is reported, and the interpretation question asks why the fixed parameter is not random in the frequentist statement.
A sample maximum from a discrete uniform distribution is used to produce a set of plausible upper endpoints.
A two-sided test at level alpha is compared with whether the null value lies inside the corresponding 100(1-alpha)% confidence interval.
More than two treatment groups are compared, and the question asks whether their population means are all equal.
Several group means are compared and someone proposes running all pairwise t-tests without adjustment.
The total variation in responses is decomposed into variation between group means and variation within groups.
There are k groups and N total observations, and the question asks for the degrees of freedom in the ANOVA table.
The test statistic is formed as a ratio of the treatment mean square to the error mean square.
The validity of the classical one-way ANOVA procedure is questioned for data from several groups.
Before interpreting a multi-group mean comparison, the problem asks whether the group variances can reasonably be treated as equal.
Several normal populations are compared, but the question is about equality of their variances rather than their means.
The global ANOVA test rejects equality of all means, and the next task is to determine which pairs of means differ.
All pairwise comparisons among group means are desired while controlling the family-wise error rate.
A post-test is requested that is less conservative than Tukey's method for comparing ordered treatment means.
The ANOVA F statistic is not large enough to reject; the interpretation asks whether this proves all treatment means are equal.
The treatment groups have unequal sample sizes, and the question asks whether the ANOVA table still tracks between and within variation.
Responses are modeled as an overall mean plus a treatment effect plus random error.
After fitting an ANOVA model, residual plots are used to judge normality and constant variance assumptions.
Two categorical factors are varied in an experiment and the task asks about the separate effect of each factor.
The effect of one factor appears to depend on the level of another factor, so the model must test more than main effects.
Experimental units are grouped into blocks to remove nuisance variability before comparing treatments.
The same subjects appear under multiple treatments, so treating all group observations as independent would be wrong.
A categorical predictor is encoded with indicator variables in a linear model, and the overall F-test compares group means.
Under the equal-variance model, the within-group mean square is used as the estimate of the common error variance.
The problem asks to verify that total sum of squares equals treatment sum of squares plus error sum of squares.
Before running a multi-treatment experiment, the researcher asks how many observations per group are needed to detect a relevant mean difference.
Several independent groups are compared, normality is doubtful, and the question asks for a rank-based alternative to one-way ANOVA.
The global test is statistically significant, but the engineering question asks whether the observed treatment differences are large enough to matter.
A small P-value is reported, and a student claims it is the probability that the null hypothesis is true.
A test returns a large P-value, and the report wants to say that the null hypothesis has been proven correct.
The null hypothesis is actually true, but the testing rule rejects it.
The alternative condition is true, but the test fails to reject the null hypothesis.
The task asks for the probability that a fixed rejection rule detects a specified alternative value.
With sample size fixed, making the rejection threshold more stringent lowers false positives but can also make detection harder.
A huge sample makes a tiny mean difference statistically significant, but the engineer asks whether the difference matters in practice.
A regression output has a large R squared, and the question asks what that quantity actually measures.
A model has R squared near one, and someone concludes future predictions must be accurate for all new x values.
Two variables show strong sample correlation, and the question asks whether one variable has been shown to cause the other.
A fitted model passes a significance test, and the report claims the assumed model is therefore the true data-generating mechanism.
A two-sided confidence interval for a mean difference barely contains zero, and the question asks what that implies for a two-sided test at the matching level.
The scientific claim is directional, but the proposed test rejects for large deviations in either direction.
The wording says the manufacturer wants evidence to support an improvement, and the task asks where the improvement claim belongs.
The prompt mixes a sample mean and a population mean, and the task asks which one is random before observing data.
A t critical value is looked up using only the significance level, ignoring the sample size.
A small-sample t procedure is proposed for highly skewed data with outliers and no normality justification.
A pooled two-sample t-test is proposed even though the sample spreads are very different and no equal-variance assumption is justified.
Measurements before and after treatment on the same units are analyzed as if they came from two unrelated groups.
Many hypotheses are tested at the 5 percent level, and the question asks why at least one small P-value may appear by chance.
A high-degree polynomial fits the observed points extremely well, but the question warns about poor generalization.
A regression line is used to predict far outside the range of observed x values.
An observational dataset shows an association, but the treatment was not randomly assigned.
A calculation treats repeated measurements as independent even though they were taken from the same machine in a time-ordered run.
